points: Sound is a form of energy produced by vibrating objects — all sound production involves rapid to-and-fro (vibratory) motion; it requires a material medium (solid, liquid, or gas) and cannot travel through vacuum.

Sound travels as a longitudinal mechanical wave — a series of alternating compressions (high pressure/density regions) and rarefactions (low pressure/density regions); it is the energy/disturbance that propagates, not the medium particles.
The wave equation v = λν (Speed = Wavelength × Frequency) governs sound; speed depends on the medium and its temperature — greatest in solids, least in gases; at 22°C in air, speed ≈ 344 m s⁻¹.

A sound wave is characterised by wavelength (λ — distance between two consecutive compressions or rarefactions), frequency (ν — oscillations per second, Hz, T = 1/ν), and amplitude (A — maximum displacement from mean position).

Pitch is determined by frequency (higher frequency → higher pitch); loudness is determined by amplitude (larger amplitude → louder sound; intensity = sound energy per second per unit area); quality/timbre distinguishes sounds of the same pitch and loudness.
Sound of a single frequency is a tone; a mixture of frequencies is a note. Speed of sound in a medium is the same for all frequencies.

summary.category: Reflection of Sound — Echo and Reverberation
points: Sound follows the same laws of reflection as light: angle of incidence = angle of reflection, both in the same plane. A reflecting surface must be large and at least 17.2 m away (at 22°C) for a distinct echo — since the brain retains sound for 0.1 s; distance = (v × t) / 2.

Reverberation is the persistence of sound in an enclosed space due to repeated reflections; reduced by covering walls and ceilings with sound-absorbent materials (fibreboard, rough plaster, draperies).
Multiple reflection is used in megaphones, stethoscopes, and curved concert hall ceilings to direct or distribute sound effectively.

summary.category: Range of Hearing and Applications of Ultrasound
points: Human audible range: 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz. Infrasound (< 20 Hz): produced by whales, elephants, rhinos; also generated by earthquakes. Ultrasound (> 20 kHz): produced by bats, dolphins, porpoises.

Ultrasound applications: industrial cleaning, detecting flaws in metal blocks, echocardiography (heart imaging), ultrasonography (imaging internal organs and foetus), breaking kidney stones, and SONAR (underwater ranging: distance = v × t / 2).
